ud1.8:1.1:
Thus I heard:
At one time the Gracious One was dwelling near Sāvatthī, in Jeta's Wood, at Anāthapiṇḍika's monastery. Then at that time venerable Sangāmajī had arrived at Sāvatthī to see the Gracious One.
ud1.8:1.2: Venerable Sangāmajī's former wife heard: "Master Sangāmajī it seems has arrived at Sāvatthī", and taking her little boy she went to Jeta's Wood.
ud1.8:1.3: Then at that time venerable Sangāmajī was dwelling for the day sat at the root of a certain tree. Then venerable Sangāmajī's former wife went to venerable Sangāmajī, and after going, she said to venerable Sangāmajī: "I have a little son, ascetic, you must take care of me."
ud1.8:1.4: After that was said, venerable Sangāmajī was silent.
ud1.8:1.5: For a second time venerable Sangāmajī's former wife said to venerable Sangāmajī: "I have a little son, ascetic, you must take care of me."
ud1.8:1.6: For a second time venerable Sangāmajī was silent.
ud1.8:1.7: For a third time venerable Sangāmajī's former wife said to venerable Sangāmajī: "I have a little son, ascetic, you must take care of me."
ud1.8:1.8: For a third time venerable Sangāmajī was silent.
ud1.8:2.2:
Then venerable Sangāmajī's former wife, having put the boy down in front of venerable Sangāmajī, went away,
: "This is your son, ascetic, you must take care of him."
ud1.8:2.3:
But venerable Sangāmajī did not look at the boy, nor did he speak
.
ud1.8:2.4:
Then venerable Sangāmajī's former wife having gone not far away, looking round saw that venerable Sangāmajī was neither looking at the boy, nor was he speaking
. Having seen
this occured to her: "This ascetic does not even have need of a son." Therefore, after turning back and taking the boy, she went away.
ud1.8:2.5:
The Gracious One saw with the divine-eye which is purified, and surpasses that of
men, that venerable Sangāmajī's former wife had such bad manners.
ud1.8:2.6: Then the Gracious One, having understood the significance of it, on that occasion uttered this exalted utterance:
ud1.8:2.7:
"In
coming he does not rejoice, in
leaving he does not grieve,
Sangāmajī ‘Victorious in Battle’, free from the shackle: him I call a brāhmaṇa."
